title: Karate

infobox.value: [Indigenous martial arts of Ryukyu Islands](/wiki/Okinawan_martial_arts "Okinawan martial arts"), [Chinese martial arts](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts")[[1]](#cite_note-Higaonna1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-okinawa_history-2)

(空手) ([/kəˈrɑːti/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); Japanese pronunciation: [[kaɾate]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ja-Karate.oga "File:Ja-Karate.oga"); [Okinawan](/wiki/Okinawan_language "Okinawan language") pronunciation: [[kaɾati]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Okinawan "Help:IPA/Okinawan")), also **karate-do** (空手道, *Karate-dō*), is a [martial art](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts") developed in the [Ryukyu Kingdom](/wiki/Ryukyu_Kingdom "Ryukyu Kingdom"). It developed from the [indigenous Ryukyuan martial arts](/wiki/Okinawan_martial_arts "Okinawan martial arts") (called *te* (手), "hand"; *tī* in Okinawan) under the influence of [Chinese martial arts](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts").[[1]](#cite_note-Higaonna1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-okinawa_history-2) While modern karate is primarily a striking art that uses punches and kicks, traditional karate training also employs [throwing](/wiki/Throw_(grappling) "Throw (grappling)") and [joint locking](/wiki/Joint_lock "Joint lock") techniques.[[3]](#cite_note-3) A karate practitioner is called a **karate-ka** (空手家).

**Karate** (空手) ([/kəˈrɑːti/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); Japanese pronunciation: [[kaɾate]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ja-Karate.oga "File:Ja-Karate.oga"); [Okinawan](/wiki/Okinawan_language "Okinawan language") pronunciation: [[kaɾati]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Okinawan "Help:IPA/Okinawan")), also **karate-do** (空手道, *Karate-dō*), is a [martial art](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts") developed in the [Ryukyu Kingdom](/wiki/Ryukyu_Kingdom "Ryukyu Kingdom"). It developed from the [indigenous Ryukyuan martial arts](/wiki/Okinawan_martial_arts "Okinawan martial arts") (called *te* (手), "hand"; *tī* in Okinawan) under the influence of [Chinese martial arts](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts").[[1]](#cite_note-Higaonna1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-okinawa_history-2) While modern karate is primarily a striking art that uses punches and kicks, traditional karate training also employs [throwing](/wiki/Throw_(grappling) "Throw (grappling)") and [joint locking](/wiki/Joint_lock "Joint lock") techniques.[[3]](#cite_note-3) A karate practitioner is called a **karate-ka** (空手家).

Beginning in the 1300s, early [Chinese martial artists](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts") brought their techniques to Okinawa. Despite the [Ryukyu Kingdom](/wiki/Ryukyu_Kingdom "Ryukyu Kingdom") being turned into a puppet state by Japanese samurai in 1609, after the [Invasion of Ryukyu](/wiki/Invasion_of_Ryukyu "Invasion of Ryukyu"), its cultural ties to China remained strong.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) Since [Ryukyuans](/wiki/Ryukyuans "Ryukyuans") were banned from carrying swords under samurai rule, groups of young aristocrats created unarmed combat methods as a form of resistance, combining Chinese and local styles of martial arts.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) Training emphasized self-discipline.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) This blend of martial arts became known as kara-te 唐手, which translates to "Chinese hand." Initially, there were no uniforms, colored belts, ranking systems, or standardized styles.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) Many elements essential to modern karate were actually incorporated a century ago.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)

The Ryukyu Kingdom had been conquered by the Japanese [Satsuma Domain](/wiki/Satsuma_Domain "Satsuma Domain") and had become its [vassal state](/wiki/Vassal_state "Vassal state") in 1609, but was formally annexed to the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") in 1879 as [Okinawa Prefecture](/wiki/Okinawa_Prefecture "Okinawa Prefecture"). The Ryukyuan [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") ([Okinawan](/wiki/Okinawan_language "Okinawan language"): *samurē*) who had been the bearers of karate lost their privileged position, and with it, karate was in danger of losing transmission. However, karate gradually regained popularity after 1905, when it began to be taught in schools in Okinawa. During the [Taishō era](/wiki/Taish%C5%8D_era "Taishō era") (1912–1926), karate was initially introduced to mainland Japan by [Ankō Itosu](/wiki/Ank%C5%8D_Itosu "Ankō Itosu") and then by his students [Gichin Funakoshi](/wiki/Gichin_Funakoshi "Gichin Funakoshi") and [Motobu Chōki](/wiki/Motobu_Ch%C5%8Dki "Motobu Chōki"). The ultranationalistic sentiment of the 1930s affected every aspect of Japanese culture.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) To make the imported martial art more relatable, Funakoshi incorporated elements from judo, such as the training uniforms, colored belts, and ranking systems.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) Karate's popularity was initially sluggish with little exposition but when a magazine reported a story about Motobu defeating a foreign boxer in Kyoto, karate rapidly became well known throughout Japan.[[5]](#cite_note-King1925-5)

In this era of escalating [Japanese militarism](/wiki/Japanese_militarism "Japanese militarism"),[[6]](#cite_note-Miyagi-6) the name was changed from 唐手 ("Chinese hand" or "[Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") hand")[[7]](#cite_note-7) to 空手 ("empty hand") – both of which are pronounced *karate* in Japanese – to indicate that the Japanese wished to develop the combat form in Japanese style.[[8]](#cite_note-8) After World War II, Okinawa became (1945) an important United States military site and karate became popular among servicemen stationed there.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [martial arts movies](/wiki/Martial_arts_films "Martial arts films") of the 1960s and 1970s served to greatly increase the popularity of martial arts around the world, and English-speakers began to use the word *karate* in a generic way to refer to all striking-based [Asian martial arts](/wiki/Asian_martial_arts "Asian martial arts").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Karate schools (*[dōjōs](/wiki/D%C5%8Dj%C5%8D "Dōjō")*) began appearing around the world, catering to those with casual interest as well as those seeking a deeper study of the art.

Karate-do, like most Japanese martial arts, is considered to be not only about fighting techniques, but also about spiritual cultivation.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Many karate schools and *dōjōs* have established rules called *[dōjō kun](/wiki/D%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_kun "Dōjō kun")*, which emphasize the perfection of character, the importance of effort, and respect for courtesy. Karate featured at the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") after its inclusion at the Games was supported by the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee"). Web Japan (sponsored by the [Japanese Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Japanese_Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs "Japanese Ministry of Foreign Affairs")) claims that karate has 50 million practitioners worldwide,[[14]](#cite_note-14) while the [World Karate Federation](/wiki/World_Karate_Federation "World Karate Federation") claims there are 100 million practitioners around the world.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Originally in Okinawa during the Ryukyu Kingdom period, there existed an indigenous Ryukyuan martial art called *te* (Okinawan:*tī*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'hand'). Furthermore, in the 19th century, a Chinese-derived martial art called *tōde* (Okinawan: *tōdī*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Tang hand') emerged. According to Gichin Funakoshi, a distinction between *Okinawa-te* and *tōde* existed in the late 19th century.[[16]](#cite_note-Funakoshi1935-16) With the emergence of *tōde*, it is thought that *te* also came to be called *Okinawa-te* (Okinawan: *Uchinādī*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Okinawa hand'). However, this distinction gradually became blurred with the decline of *Okinawa-te*.

Around 1905, when karate began to be taught in public schools in Okinawa, *tōde* was read [kun'yomi](/wiki/Kun%27yomi "Kun'yomi") and called *karate* (唐手, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Tang hand') in the Japanese style. Both *tōde* and *karate* are written in the same [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") meaning "Tang/China hand," but the former is [on'yomi](/wiki/On%27yomi "On'yomi") (Chinese reading) and the latter is kun'yomi (Japanese reading). Since the distinction between *Okinawa-te* and *tōde* was already blurred at that time, *karate* was used to encompass both. *Kara* (から) is a kun'yomi for the character 唐, read as *tō* (とう) in [on'yomi](/wiki/On%27yomi "On'yomi"). Read aloud in kun'yomi, *kara* also refers to the Korean state [Kaya](/wiki/Kaya_confederacy#Names "Kaya confederacy") (加羅) and later included things deriving from China (specifically from the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty")).[[17]](#cite_note-17) Therefore, *tōde* and *karate* (Tang hand) differ in the scope of meaning of the words.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Japan sent envoys to the Tang dynasty and introduced much Chinese culture. Gichin Funakoshi proposed that *tōde*/karate may have been used instead of *te*, as Tang became a synonym for luxury imported goods.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

According to Gichin Funakoshi, the word pronounced karate (から手) existed in the Ryukyu Kingdom period, but it is unclear whether it meant Tang hand (唐手) or empty hand (空手).[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The Chinese origins of karate were increasingly viewed with suspicion due to rising tensions between China and Japan and as well as the looming threat of a full-scale war between the two countries.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) In 1933, the Japanese character for karate was altered to a homophone— a word pronounced identically but with a different meaning. Thus, "Chinese hand" was replaced with "empty hand."[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)

But this name change did not immediately spread among Okinawan karate practitioners. There were many karate practitioners, such as [Chōjun Miyagi](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Djun_Miyagi "Chōjun Miyagi"), who still used *te* in everyday conversation until World War II.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

When karate was first taught in mainland Japan in the 1920s, Gichin Funakoshi and Motobu Chōki used the name *karate-jutsu* (唐手術, lit. 'Tang hand art') along with karate.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) The word *jutsu* (術) means art or technique, and in those days it was often used as a suffix to the name of each martial art, as in *[jujutsu](/wiki/Jujutsu "Jujutsu")* and *[kenjutsu](/wiki/Kenjutsu "Kenjutsu")* (swordsmanship).[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The first documented use of a [homophone](/wiki/Homophone "Homophone") of the [logogram](/wiki/Logogram "Logogram") pronounced *kara* by replacing the [Chinese character](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters") meaning "Tang dynasty" with the character meaning "empty" took place in *Karate Kumite* (空手組手) written in August 1905 by [Chōmo Hanashiro](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dmo_Hanashiro "Chōmo Hanashiro") (1869–1945).[[25]](#cite_note-25) In mainland Japan, karate (空手, empty hand) gradually began to be used from the writings of Gichin Funakoshi and Motobu Chōki in the 1920s.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 1929 the Karate Study Group of [Keio University](/wiki/Keio_University "Keio University") (Instructor Gichin Funakoshi) used this term in reference to the concept of emptiness in the [Heart Sutra](/wiki/Heart_Sutra "Heart Sutra"), and this terminology was later popularized, especially in Tokyo. There is also a theory that the background for this name change was the worsening of Japan-China relations at the time.[[28]](#cite_note-paltz-28)

On 25 October 1936 a roundtable meeting of karate masters was held in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, and it was officially resolved to use the name karate (empty hand) in the sense of *kūshu kūken* (空手空拳, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'without anything in the hands or fists').[[29]](#cite_note-29) To commemorate this day, the Okinawa Prefectural Assembly passed a resolution in 2005 to decide 25 October as "Karate Day."[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Another nominal development is the addition of *dō* (道; どう) to the end of the word karate. *Dō* is a suffix having numerous meanings including road, path, route and way.[[31]](#cite_note-31) It is used in many martial arts that survived Japan's [transition](/wiki/Meiji_period "Meiji period") from [feudal culture](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") to [modern times](/wiki/Economy_of_Japan "Economy of Japan"). It implies that these arts are not just fighting systems but contain spiritual elements when promoted as disciplines.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In this context *dō* is usually translated as "the way of …". Examples include [aikido](/wiki/Aikido "Aikido"), judo, [kyūdō](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABd%C5%8D "Kyūdō") and [kendo](/wiki/Kendo "Kendo"). Thus karatedō is more than just empty hand techniques. It is "the way of the empty hand".[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Since the 1980s the term karate (カラテ) has been written in [katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana") instead of Chinese characters, mainly by [Kyokushin](/wiki/Kyokushin "Kyokushin") Karate (founder: [Masutatsu Oyama](/wiki/Masutatsu_Oyama "Masutatsu Oyama")).[[34]](#cite_note-34) In Japan, katakana is mainly used for foreign words, giving Kyokushin Karate a modern and new impression.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origin")]

There are several theories regarding the origins of karate, but the main ones are as follows.

#### Theory of development from *mēkata*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Theory of development from mēkata")]

In Okinawa there was an ancient martial dance called *mēkata* (舞方). The dancers danced to the accompaniment of songs and *[sanshin](/wiki/Sanshin "Sanshin")* music, similar to karate kata. In the Okinawan countryside, *mēkata* remained until the early 20th century. There is a theory that from this *mēkata* with martial elements, *te* (Okinawan:*tī*, hand) was born and developed into karate. This theory is advocated by [Ankō Asato](/wiki/Ank%C5%8D_Asato "Ankō Asato") and his student Gichin Funakoshi.[[35]](#cite_note-Asato1914-35)

#### Theory of introduction by thirty-six families from Min

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Theory of introduction by thirty-six families from Min")]

It is said that in 1392 a group of professional people known as the "[Thirty-six families from Min](/wiki/Thirty-six_families_from_Min "Thirty-six families from Min")" migrated to [Kume Village](/wiki/Kumemura "Kumemura") (now Kume, Naha City) in Naha from Fujian Province in the Ming Dynasty at that time. They brought with them advanced learning and skills to Ryukyu, and there is a theory that Chinese kenpō, the origin of karate, was also brought to Ryukyu at this time.

There is also the "[Keichō](/wiki/Keich%C5%8D "Keichō") import theory," which states that karate was brought to Ryukyu after the invasion of Ryukyu by the [Satsuma Domain](/wiki/Satsuma_Domain "Satsuma Domain") (Keichō 14, 1609), as well as the theory that it was introduced by [Kōshōkun](/wiki/K%C5%8Dsh%C5%8Dkun "Kōshōkun") (Okinawan: Kūsankū) based on the description in *Ōshima Writing*.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

#### Other theories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other theories")]

There are also other theories, such as that it developed from Okinawan [sumo](/wiki/Sumo "Sumo") (*shima*) or that it originated from [jujutsu](/wiki/Jujutsu "Jujutsu"), which had been introduced from Japan.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Okinawa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Okinawa")]

Main article: [Okinawan martial arts](/wiki/Okinawan_martial_arts "Okinawan martial arts")

#### 15th–17th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 15th–17th centuries")]

[![King Shō Shin](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/King_Sho_Shin.jpg/250px-King_Sho_Shin.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Sho_Shin.jpg)

King Shō Shin

The reason for the development of unarmed combat techniques in Ryukyu has conventionally been attributed to a policy of banning weapons, which is said to have been implemented on two occasions. The first was during the reign of King [Shō Shin](/wiki/Sh%C5%8D_Shin "Shō Shin") (1476–1526; r. 1477–1527), when weapons were collected from all over the country and strictly controlled by the royal government. The second time was after the invasion of Ryukyu by the Satsuma Domain in 1609. Through the two policies, the popular belief that Ryukyuan samurai, who were deprived of their weapons, developed karate to compete with Satsuma's samurai has traditionally been referred to as if it were a historical fact.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

But in recent years many researchers have questioned the causal relationship between the policy of banning weapons and the development of karate.[[39]](#cite_note-Kinjo2011-39) For example, as the basis for King Shō Shin's policy of banning weapons, an inscription on the parapet of the main hall of Shuri Castle (百浦添欄干之銘, 1509), which states that "swords, bows and arrows are to be piled up exclusively as weapons of national defense,"[[40]](#cite_note-40) has been conventionally interpreted as meaning "weapons were collected and sealed in a warehouse." However, in recent years, researchers of Okinawan studies have pointed out that the correct interpretation is that "swords, bows and arrows were collected and used as weapons of the state."[[41]](#cite_note-41)

It is also known that the policy of banning weapons (a 1613 notice to the Ryukyu royal government), which is said to have been implemented by the Satsuma Domain, only prohibited the carrying of swords and other weapons, but not their possession, and was a relatively lax regulation. This notice stated, "(1) The possession of guns is prohibited. (2) The possession of weapons owned privately by princes, three magistrates, and samurai is permitted. (3) Weapons must be repaired in Satsuma through the magistrate's office of Satsuma. (4) Swords must be reported to the magistrate's office of Satsuma for approval."[[42]](#cite_note-42) It did not prohibit the possession of weapons (except guns) or even their practice. In fact, even after subjugation to the Satsuma Domain, a number of Ryukyuan masters of swordsmanship, spearmanship, archery, and other arts are known. Therefore, some researchers criticize the theory that karate developed due to the policy of banning weapons as "a rumor on the street with no basis at all."[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Karate began as a common fighting system known as *te* (Okinawan: *tī*) among the Ryukyuan samurai class. There were few formal styles of *te,* but rather many practitioners with their own methods. One surviving example is [Motobu Udundī](/wiki/Motobu-ry%C5%AB "Motobu-ryū") ([lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Motobu Palace Hand'), which has been handed down to this day in the [Motobu family](/wiki/House_of_Motobu "House of Motobu"), one of the branches of the former Ryukyu royal family.[[44]](#cite_note-44) In the 16th century, the Ryukyuan history book "[Kyūyō](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABy%C5%8D "Kyūyō")" (球陽, established around 1745) mentions that [Kyō Ahagon Jikki](/w/index.php?title=%E4%BA%AC%E9%98%BF%E6%B3%A2%E6%A0%B9%E5%AE%9F%E5%9F%BA&action=edit&redlink=1 "京阿波根実基 (page does not exist)")[[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%AC%E9%98%BF%E6%B3%A2%E6%A0%B9%E5%AE%9F%E5%9F%BA "ja:京阿波根実基")], a favored retainer of King Shō Shin, used a martial art called "karate" (空手, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'empty hand') to smash both legs of an assassin. This karate is thought to refer to *te*, not today's karate, and Ankō Asato introduces Kyō Ahagon as a "prominent martial artist."[[35]](#cite_note-Asato1914-35)

#### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 18th century")]

However, some believe that Kyō Ahagon's anecdote is a half-legend and that it is unclear whether he was actually a *te* master. In the 18th century, the names of Nishinda *Uēkata*, Gushikawa *Uēkata*, and Chōken Makabe are known as masters of *te*.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Nishinda *Uēkata* and Gushikawa *Uēkata* were martial artists active during the reign of King [Shō Kei](/wiki/Sh%C5%8D_Kei "Shō Kei") (reigned 1713–1751). Nishinda *Uēkata* was good at spear as well as *te*, and Gushikawa *Uēkata* was also good at wooden sword (swordsmanship).[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Chōken Makabe was a man of the late 18th century. His light stature and jumping ability gave him the nickname "Makabe *Chān-gwā*" ([lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'little fighting cock'), as he was like a *chān* (fighting cock). The ceiling of his house is said to have been marked by his kicking foot.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

It is known that in "Ōshima Writing" (1762), written by Yoshihiro Tobe, a Confucian scholar of the [Tosa Domain](/wiki/Tosa_Domain "Tosa Domain"), who interviewed Ryukyuan samurai who had drifted to Tosa (present-day [Kōchi Prefecture](/wiki/K%C5%8Dchi_Prefecture "Kōchi Prefecture")), there is a description of a martial art called *kumiai-jutsu* (組合術) performed by [Kōshōkun](/wiki/K%C5%8Dsh%C5%8Dkun "Kōshōkun") (Okinawan:Kūsankū). It is believed that Kōshōkun may have been a military officer on a mission from Qing that visited Ryukyu in 1756, and some believe that karate originated with Kōshōkun.

In addition, the will (Part I: 1778, Part II: 1783) of Ryukyuan samurai Aka Pēchin Chokushki (1721–1784) mentions the name of a martial art called *karamutō* (からむとう), along with Japanese [Jigen-ryū](/wiki/Jigen-ry%C5%AB "Jigen-ryū") swordsmanship and [jujutsu](/wiki/Jujutsu "Jujutsu"), indicating that Ryukyuan samurai practiced these arts in the 18th century.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In 1609, the Japanese [Satsuma Domain](/wiki/Satsuma_Domain "Satsuma Domain") invaded Ryukyu and Ryukyu became its vassal state, but it continued to pay tribute to the Ming and Qing Dynasties in China. At the time, China had implemented a policy of [sea ban](/wiki/Sea_ban "Sea ban") and only traded with tributary countries, so the Satsuma Domain wanted Ryukyu to continue its tribute to benefit from it.

The envoys of the tribute mission were chosen from among the samurai class of Ryukyu, and they went to [Fuzhou](/wiki/Fuzhou "Fuzhou") in Fujian and stayed there for six months to a year and a half. Government-funded and privately funded foreign students were also sent to study in Beijing or Fuzhou for several years. Some of these envoys and students studied Chinese martial arts in China. The styles of Chinese martial arts they studied are not known for certain, but it is assumed that they studied [Fujian White Crane](/wiki/Fujian_White_Crane "Fujian White Crane") and other styles from Fujian Province.

Sōryo Tsūshin (monk Tsūshin), active during the reign of King Shō Kei, was a monk who went to the Qing Dynasty to study Chinese martial arts and was reportedly one of the best martial artists of his time in Ryukyu.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

#### 19th and early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 19th and early 20th century")]

It is not known when the name *tōde* (唐手, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Tang hand') first came into use in the Ryukyu Kingdom, but according to Ankō Asato, it was popularized from [Kanga Sakugawa](/wiki/Kanga_Sakugawa "Kanga Sakugawa") (1786–1867), who was nicknamed "Tōde Sakugawa."[[35]](#cite_note-Asato1914-35) Sakugawa was a samurai from Shuri who traveled to Qing China to learn Chinese martial arts. The martial arts he mastered were new and different from te. As *tōde* was spread by Sakugawa, traditional *te* became distinguished as *Okinawa-te* (沖縄手, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Okinawa hand'), and gradually faded away as it merged with *tōde*.

It is generally believed that today's karate is a result of the synthesis of *te* (*Okinawa-te*) and *tōde*. Funakoshi writes, "In the early modern era, when China was highly revered, many martial artists traveled to China to practice Chinese kenpo, and added it to the ancient kenpo, the so-called 'Okinawa-te'. After further study, they discarded the disadvantages of both, adopted their advantages, and added more subtlety, and karate was born."[[16]](#cite_note-Funakoshi1935-16)

Early styles of karate are often generalized as [Shuri-te](/wiki/Shuri-te "Shuri-te"), [Naha-te](/wiki/Naha-te "Naha-te"), and [Tomari-te](/wiki/Tomari-te "Tomari-te"), named after the three cities from which they emerged.[[50]](#cite_note-Higaonna_1985_19-50) Each area and its teachers had particular kata, techniques, and principles that distinguished their local version of *te* from the others.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Karate_ShuriCastle.jpg/250px-Karate_ShuriCastle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karate_ShuriCastle.jpg)

Karate training in front of [Shuri Castle](/wiki/Shuri_Castle "Shuri Castle") in [Naha](/wiki/Naha "Naha") (1938)

Around the 1820s, [Matsumura Sōkon](/wiki/Matsumura_S%C5%8Dkon "Matsumura Sōkon") (1809–1899) began teaching *Okinawa-te*.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Matsumura was, according to one theory, a student of Sakugawa. Matsumura's style later became the origin of many [Shuri-te](/wiki/Shuri-te "Shuri-te") schools.

[Itosu Ankō](/wiki/Itosu_Ank%C5%8D "Itosu Ankō") (1831–1915) studied under Matsumura and Bushi Nagahama of [Naha-te](/wiki/Naha-te "Naha-te").[[52]](#cite_note-52) He created the *[Pin'an](/wiki/Pinan "Pinan")* forms ("*Heian*" in Japanese) which are simplified kata for beginning students. In 1905, Itosu helped to get karate introduced into Okinawa's public schools. These forms were taught to children at the elementary school level. Itosu's influence in karate is broad. The forms he created are common across nearly all styles of karate. His students became some of the most well-known karate masters, including [Motobu Chōyū](/wiki/Motobu_Ch%C5%8Dy%C5%AB "Motobu Chōyū"), [Motobu Chōki](/wiki/Motobu_Ch%C5%8Dki "Motobu Chōki"), [Yabu Kentsū](/wiki/Yabu_Kents%C5%AB "Yabu Kentsū"), [Hanashiro Chōmo](/wiki/Hanashiro_Chomo "Hanashiro Chomo"), [Gichin Funakoshi](/wiki/Gichin_Funakoshi "Gichin Funakoshi") and [Kenwa Mabuni](/wiki/Kenwa_Mabuni "Kenwa Mabuni"). Itosu is sometimes referred to as "the Grandfather of Modern Karate."[[53]](#cite_note-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Karate_in_Naha_before_the_war.jpg/250px-Karate_in_Naha_before_the_war.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karate_in_Naha_before_the_war.jpg)

Karate in [Naha](/wiki/Naha "Naha") before the war (before 1946)

In 1881, [Higaonna Kanryō](/wiki/Higaonna_Kanry%C5%8D "Higaonna Kanryō") returned from China after years of instruction with [Ryu Ryu Ko](/wiki/Ryu_Ryu_Ko "Ryu Ryu Ko") and founded what would become [Naha-te](/wiki/Naha-te "Naha-te"). One of his students was the founder of [Gojū-ryū](/wiki/Goj%C5%AB-ry%C5%AB "Gojū-ryū"), [Chōjun Miyagi](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Djun_Miyagi "Chōjun Miyagi"). Chōjun Miyagi taught such well-known karateka as [Seko Higa](/wiki/Seko_Higa "Seko Higa") (who also trained with Higaonna), [Meitoku Yagi](/wiki/Meitoku_Yagi "Meitoku Yagi"), [Miyazato Ei'ichi](/wiki/Miyazato_Ei%27ichi "Miyazato Ei'ichi"), and [Seikichi Toguchi](/wiki/Seikichi_Toguchi "Seikichi Toguchi"), and for a very brief time near the end of his life, An'ichi Miyagi (a teacher claimed by [Morio Higaonna](/wiki/Morio_Higaonna "Morio Higaonna")).

In addition to the three early *te* styles of karate a fourth Okinawan influence is that of [Uechi Kanbun](/wiki/Kanbun_Uechi "Kanbun Uechi") (1877–1948). At the age of 20 he went to [Fuzhou](/wiki/Fuzhou "Fuzhou") in Fujian Province, China, to escape Japanese military conscription. While there he studied under Shū Shiwa (Chinese: *Zhou Zihe* 周子和 1874–1926).[[54]](#cite_note-54) He was a leading figure of Chinese [Nanpa Shorin-ken](/wiki/Nam_Pai_Chuan "Nam Pai Chuan") style at that time.[[55]](#cite_note-55) He later developed his own style of [Uechi-ryū](/wiki/Uechi-ry%C5%AB "Uechi-ryū") karate based on the [Sanchin](/wiki/Sanchin "Sanchin"), [Seisan](/wiki/Seisan "Seisan"), and Sanseiryu kata that he had studied in China.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Japan")]

Main article: [Karate in Japan](/wiki/Karate_in_Japan "Karate in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Funakoshi_Gichin.jpg/250px-Funakoshi_Gichin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Funakoshi_Gichin.jpg)

Gichin Funakoshi

When [Shō Tai](/wiki/Sh%C5%8D_Tai "Shō Tai"), the last king of the Ryūkyū Kingdom, was ordered to move to Tokyo in 1879, he was accompanied by prominent karate masters such as [Ankō Asato](/wiki/Ank%C5%8D_Asato "Ankō Asato") and Chōfu Kyan (father of [Chōtoku Kyan](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dtoku_Kyan "Chōtoku Kyan")). It is unknown if they taught karate to the Japanese in Tokyo, although there are records that Kyan taught his son karate.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In 1908, students from the Okinawa Prefectural Middle School gave a karate demonstration at [Butokuden](/wiki/Dai_Nippon_Butoku_Kai "Dai Nippon Butoku Kai") in Kyoto, which was also witnessed by [Kanō Jigorō](/wiki/Kan%C5%8D_Jigor%C5%8D "Kanō Jigorō") (founder of judo).

In May 1922, Gichin Funakoshi (founder of [Shotokan](/wiki/Shotokan "Shotokan")) presented pictures of karate on two hanging scrolls at the first Physical Education Exhibition in Tokyo.[[58]](#cite_note-Funakoshi1956-58) The following June, Funakoshi was invited to the [Kodokan](/wiki/Kodokan_Judo_Institute "Kodokan Judo Institute") to give a karate demonstration in front of Jigoro Kano and other judo experts. This was the beginning of the full-scale introduction of karate in Tokyo.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Motobu_king1925.jpg/250px-Motobu_king1925.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motobu_king1925.jpg)

Illustration depicting a match between Motobu Chōki and a foreign boxer

In November 1922, [Motobu Chōki](/wiki/Motobu_Ch%C5%8Dki "Motobu Chōki") (founder of [Motobu-ryū](/wiki/Motobu-ry%C5%AB "Motobu-ryū")) participated in a judo versus boxing match in Kyoto, defeating a foreign boxer. The match was featured in Japan's largest magazine "[King](/w/index.php?title=%E3%82%AD%E3%83%B3%E3%82%B0_(%E9%9B%91%E8%AA%8C)&action=edit&redlink=1 "キング (雑誌) (page does not exist)")[[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%AD%E3%83%B3%E3%82%B0_(%E9%9B%91%E8%AA%8C) "ja:キング (雑誌)")]," which had a circulation of about one million at the time, and karate and Motobu's name became instantly known throughout Japan.[[5]](#cite_note-King1925-5)

In 1922, Funakoshi published the first book on karate,[[59]](#cite_note-59) and in 1926 Motobu published the first technical book on kumite (sparring).[[60]](#cite_note-60) As karate's popularity grew, karate clubs were established one after another in Japanese universities with Funakoshi and Motobu as instructors.[[61]](#cite_note-Nakasone1934-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In the Showa era (1926–1989), other Okinawan karate masters also came to mainland Japan to teach karate. These included [Kenwa Mabuni](/wiki/Kenwa_Mabuni "Kenwa Mabuni"), [Chōjun Miyagi](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Djun_Miyagi "Chōjun Miyagi"), [Kanken Tōyama](/wiki/Kanken_T%C5%8Dyama "Kanken Tōyama"), and [Kanbun Uechi](/wiki/Kanbun_Uechi "Kanbun Uechi").

With the rise of [militarism](/wiki/Militarism "Militarism") in Japan, some karate masters gradually came to consider the name karate (唐手, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") '[Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") hand') undesirable. The name karate (空手, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'empty hand') had already been used by [Chōmo Hanashiro](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dmo_Hanashiro "Chōmo Hanashiro") in Okinawa in 1905,[[63]](#cite_note-63) and Funakoshi decided to use this name as well. In addition, the name *karatedō* (唐手道, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'the way of the Tang hand'), which was already used by the karate club of [Tokyo Imperial University](/wiki/Tokyo_Imperial_University "Tokyo Imperial University") (now the University of Tokyo) in 1929 by adding the suffix *dō* (道, way) to karate,[[64]](#cite_note-64) was also used by Funakoshi, who decided to use the name *karatedō* (空手道, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'the way of the empty hand') in the same way.[[16]](#cite_note-Funakoshi1935-16)

The *dō* suffix implies that *karatedō* is a path to self-knowledge, not just a study of the technical aspects of fighting. Like most martial arts practised in Japan, karate made its transition from -*jutsu* to -*dō* around the beginning of the 20th century. The "*dō*" in "karate-dō" sets it apart from karate-*jutsu*, as [aikido](/wiki/Aikido "Aikido") is distinguished from [aikijutsu](/wiki/Aikijutsu "Aikijutsu"), judo from [jujutsu](/wiki/Jujutsu "Jujutsu"), [kendo](/wiki/Kendo "Kendo") from [kenjutsu](/wiki/Kenjutsu "Kenjutsu") and [iaido](/wiki/Iaido "Iaido") from [iaijutsu](/wiki/Iaijutsu "Iaijutsu").

In 1933, karate was officially recognized as a Japanese martial art by the [Dai Nippon Butoku Kai](/wiki/Dai_Nippon_Butoku_Kai "Dai Nippon Butoku Kai"), but initially belonged to the [jujutsu](/wiki/Jujutsu "Jujutsu") division and title examinations were conducted by jujutsu masters.

By 1935 Funakoshi had changed the names of many kata and karate itself. Funakoshi's motivation was that the names of many of the traditional kata were unintelligible, and that it would be inappropriate to use the Chinese style names to teach karate as a Japanese martial art.[[65]](#cite_note-65) He also said that the kata had to be simplified to spread karate as a form of physical education, so some of the kata were modified.[[66]](#cite_note-66) He always referred to what he taught as simply karate, but in 1936 he built a dōjō in Tokyo and the style he left behind is usually called [Shotokan](/wiki/Shotokan "Shotokan") after this dōjō. *Shoto*, meaning "pine wave", was Funakoshi's pen name and *kan* meaning "hall".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Masters_of_Karate.jpg/250px-Masters_of_Karate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Masters_of_Karate.jpg)

Masters of karate in Tokyo (c. 1930s), from left to right, [Kanken Toyama](/wiki/Kanken_Toyama "Kanken Toyama"), [Hironori Otsuka](/wiki/Hironori_Otsuka "Hironori Otsuka"), Takeshi Shimoda, [Gichin Funakoshi](/wiki/Gichin_Funakoshi "Gichin Funakoshi"), [Chōki Motobu](/wiki/Motobu_Ch%C5%8Dki "Motobu Chōki"), [Kenwa Mabuni](/wiki/Kenwa_Mabuni "Kenwa Mabuni"), Genwa Nakasone, and [Shinken Taira](/wiki/Shinken_Taira "Shinken Taira")

On 25 October 1936, a roundtable meeting of karate masters was held in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, where it was officially decided to change the name of karate from karate (Tang hand) to karate (empty hand). In attendance were Chōmo Hanashiro, Chōki Motobu, Chōtoku Kyan, [Jūhatsu Kyoda](/wiki/Juhatsu_Kyoda "Juhatsu Kyoda"), [Chōjun Miyagi](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Djun_Miyagi "Chōjun Miyagi"), [Shinpan Gusukuma](/wiki/Shinpan_Gusukuma "Shinpan Gusukuma"), and [Chōshin Chibana](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dshin_Chibana "Chōshin Chibana"). In 2005, the [Okinawa Prefectural Assembly](/wiki/Okinawa_Prefectural_Assembly "Okinawa Prefectural Assembly") passed a resolution to commemorate this decision by designating 25 October as "Karate Day."[[67]](#cite_note-67)

The modernization and systemization of karate in Japan also included the adoption of the white uniform that consisted of the *[dogi](/wiki/Dogi "Dogi")* or *[keikogi](/wiki/Keikogi "Keikogi")*—mostly called just [karategi](/wiki/Karategi "Karategi")—and coloured belt ranks. Both of these innovations were originated and popularized by [Jigoro Kano](/wiki/Jigoro_Kano "Jigoro Kano"), the founder of judo and one of the men Funakoshi consulted in his efforts to modernize karate.

At that time, there was almost no kumite training in karate, and kata training was the main focus.[[68]](#cite_note-Konishi1977-68) There were also no matches. However, at that time, judo and [kendo](/wiki/Kendo "Kendo") matches were already being held in mainland Japan, and *[randori](/wiki/Randori "Randori")* (乱取り; [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'free-style practice') practice was also being actively practiced, the young people in mainland Japan gradually became dissatisfied with kata-only practice.[[68]](#cite_note-Konishi1977-68)

In pre–World War II Okinawa, karateka practiced *iri kumi* ([Okinawan](/wiki/Okinawan_language "Okinawan language") for kumite) allowing all kinds of techniques (strikes, choke holds, joint locks, etc.) but in a controlled manner to not injure the opponent when aiming to vital areas.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Despite sparring was originally an unnoticed form of practice for senior students, there were no "contests" until Western-style competitions were introduced to Japan.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

[Gichin Funakoshi](/wiki/Gichin_Funakoshi "Gichin Funakoshi") stated, "There are no contests in karate."[[71]](#cite_note-71) [Shigeru Egami](/wiki/Shigeru_Egami "Shigeru Egami") relates that, during his visit to Okinawa in 1940, he heard some karateka were ousted from their *dōjō* because they adopted sparring after having learned it in Tokyo. In the early 1930s, pre-arranged sparring was introduced and developed, and finally a few years later free sparring was permitted for Shotokan students.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

According to [Yasuhiro Konishi](/wiki/Yasuhiro_Konishi "Yasuhiro Konishi"), kata-only training was often criticized by the leading judo practitioners of the time, such as [Shuichi Nagaoka](/wiki/Shuichi_Nagaoka "Shuichi Nagaoka") and [Hajime Isogai](/wiki/Hajime_Isogai "Hajime Isogai"), who said, "The karate you do cannot be understood from kata alone, so why don't you try a little more so that the general public can understand it?"[[68]](#cite_note-Konishi1977-68) Against the backdrop of these complaints and criticisms, young people such as [Hironori Ōtsuka](/wiki/Hironori_%C5%8Ctsuka "Hironori Ōtsuka") and Konishi devised their own kumite and kumite matches, which are the prototypes of today's kumite.[[68]](#cite_note-Konishi1977-68)[[73]](#cite_note-73) Motobu's emphasis on kumite attracted Ōtsuka and Konishi, who later studied Okinawan kumite under him.[[68]](#cite_note-Konishi1977-68)

After World War II, karate activities were temporarily stalled due to the "Notice Banning Judo, Kendo, and Other Martial Arts" issued by the Ministry of Education under the directive of the [Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers](/wiki/Supreme_Commander_for_the_Allied_Powers "Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers"). However, because this notice did not include the word "karate," it was interpreted by the Ministry of Education that karate was not prohibited, and karate was able to resume its activities earlier than other martial arts.

A new form of karate called [Kyokushin](/wiki/Kyokushin "Kyokushin") was formally founded in 1957 by [Masutatsu Oyama](/wiki/Masutatsu_Oyama "Masutatsu Oyama") (who was born a Korean, Choi Yeong-Eui 최영의). Kyokushin is largely a synthesis of Shotokan and Gōjū-ryū. It teaches a curriculum that emphasizes [aliveness](/wiki/Aliveness_(martial_arts) "Aliveness (martial arts)"), physical toughness, and [full contact](/wiki/Full-contact "Full-contact") sparring. Because of its emphasis on physical, full-force [sparring](/wiki/Sparring "Sparring"), Kyokushin is now often called "[full contact karate](/wiki/Full_contact_karate "Full contact karate")", or "[Knockdown karate](/wiki/Knockdown_karate "Knockdown karate")" (after the name for its competition rules). Many other karate organizations and styles are descended from the Kyokushin curriculum.

## Practice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Practice")]

See also: [Okinawan kobudō](/wiki/Okinawan_kobud%C5%8D "Okinawan kobudō") and [Japanese martial arts § Philosophical and strategic concepts](/wiki/Japanese_martial_arts#Philosophical_and_strategic_concepts "Japanese martial arts")

Karate can be practiced as an art ([budō](/wiki/Bud%C5%8D "Budō")), [self defense](/wiki/Self-defense "Self-defense") or as a [combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport"). Traditional karate places emphasis on self-development (budō).[[74]](#cite_note-74) Modern Japanese style training emphasizes the psychological elements incorporated into a proper *kokoro* (attitude) such as perseverance, fearlessness, virtue, and leadership skills. Sport karate places emphasis on exercise and competition. Weapons are an important training activity in some styles of karate.

Karate training is commonly divided into *[kihon](/wiki/Kihon "Kihon")* (basics or fundamentals), *[kata](/wiki/Kata_(martial_arts) "Kata (martial arts)")* (forms), and *[kumite](/wiki/Kumite "Kumite")* (sparring).

### Kihon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Kihon")]

Main article: [Kihon](/wiki/Kihon "Kihon")

Kihon means basics and these form the base for everything else in the style including stances, strikes, punches, kicks and blocks. Karate styles place varying importance on kihon. Typically this is training in unison of a technique or a combination of techniques by a group of karateka. Kihon may also be prearranged drills in smaller groups or in pairs.

### Kata

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Kata")]

Main article: [Karate kata](/wiki/Karate_kata "Karate kata")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Motobu_Choki2.jpg/250px-Motobu_Choki2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motobu_Choki2.jpg)

[Chōki Motobu](/wiki/Motobu_Ch%C5%8Dki "Motobu Chōki") in Naihanchi-dachi, one of the basic karate stances

Kata ([型](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%9E%8B "wikt:型"):かた) means literally "shape" or "model." Kata is a formalized sequence of movements which represent various offensive and defensive postures. These postures are based on idealized combat applications. The applications when applied in a demonstration with real opponents is referred to as a [Bunkai](/wiki/Bunkai "Bunkai"). The Bunkai shows how every stance and movement is used. Bunkai is a useful tool to understand a kata.

To attain a formal rank the karateka must demonstrate competent performance of specific required kata for that level. The Japanese terminology for grades or ranks is commonly used. Requirements for examinations vary among schools.

### Kumite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Kumite")]

Main article: [Kumite](/wiki/Kumite "Kumite")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/%E3%81%8B%E3%82%89%E3%81%A6.jpg/250px-%E3%81%8B%E3%82%89%E3%81%A6.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E3%81%8B%E3%82%89%E3%81%A6.jpg)

[Bōgutsuki](/wiki/B%C5%8Dgutsuki_Karate "Bōgutsuki Karate"), a form of full-contact karate fought with [armour](/wiki/B%C5%8Dgu "Bōgu"), one of the competition formats for kumite

[Sparring](/wiki/Sparring "Sparring") in Karate is called kumite (組手:くみて). It literally means "meeting of hands." Kumite is practiced both as a sport and as self-defense training.
Levels of physical contact during sparring vary considerably. [Full contact karate](/wiki/Full_contact_karate "Full contact karate") has several variants. Knockdown karate (such as [Kyokushin](/wiki/Kyokushin "Kyokushin")) uses full power techniques to bring an opponent to the ground. Sparring in armour, *[bogu kumite](/wiki/Bogu_kumite "Bogu kumite")*, allows full power techniques with some safety. Sport kumite in many international competition under the [World Karate Federation](/wiki/World_Karate_Federation "World Karate Federation") is free or structured with [light contact](/wiki/Light_contact "Light contact") or [semi contact](/wiki/Semi_contact "Semi contact") and points are awarded by a referee.

In structured kumite (*yakusoku*, prearranged), two participants perform a choreographed series of techniques with one striking while the other blocks. The form ends with one devastating technique (*hito tsuki*).

In free sparring (Jiyu Kumite), the two participants have a free choice of scoring techniques. The allowed techniques and contact level are primarily determined by sport or style organization policy, but might be modified according to the age, rank and sex of the participants. Depending upon style, [take-downs](/wiki/Takedown_(grappling) "Takedown (grappling)"), [sweeps](/wiki/Sweep_(martial_arts) "Sweep (martial arts)") and in some rare cases even time-limited [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling") on the ground are also allowed.

Competition sparring (Shiai Kumite) is performed in a marked or closed area. The bout runs for a fixed time (2 to 3 minutes.) The time can run continuously (*iri kume*) or be stopped for referee judgment. In [light contact](/wiki/Light_contact "Light contact") or [semi contact](/wiki/Semi_contact "Semi contact") kumite, points are awarded based on the criteria: good form, sporting attitude, vigorous application, awareness/*[zanshin](/wiki/Zanshin "Zanshin")*, good timing and correct distance.
In full contact karate kumite, points are based on the results of the impact, rather than the formal appearance of the scoring technique.

### Dōjō Kun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Dōjō Kun")]

Main article: [Dōjō kun](/wiki/D%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_kun "Dōjō kun")

In the [bushidō](/wiki/Bushid%C5%8D "Bushidō") tradition *dōjō kun* is a set of guidelines for karateka to follow. These guidelines apply both in the *[dōjō](/wiki/D%C5%8Dj%C5%8D "Dōjō")* (training hall) and in everyday life.

### Conditioning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Conditioning")]

Okinawan karate uses supplementary training known as *[hojo undo](/wiki/Hojo_undo "Hojo undo")*. This uses simple equipment made of wood and stone. The *[makiwara](/wiki/Makiwara "Makiwara")* is a striking post. The *[nigiri game](/wiki/Hojo_undo#Nigiri_game "Hojo undo")* is a large jar used for developing grip strength. These supplementary exercises are designed to increase [strength](/wiki/Physical_strength "Physical strength"), [stamina](/wiki/Endurance "Endurance"), [speed](/wiki/Speed "Speed"), and [muscle coordination](/wiki/Motor_coordination "Motor coordination").[[75]](#cite_note-75) Sport Karate emphasizes [aerobic exercise](/wiki/Aerobic_exercise "Aerobic exercise"), [anaerobic exercise](/wiki/Anaerobic_exercise "Anaerobic exercise"), [power](/wiki/Power_(physics) "Power (physics)"), [agility](/wiki/Sport_agility "Sport agility"), [flexibility](/wiki/Flexibility_(anatomy) "Flexibility (anatomy)"), and [stress management](/wiki/Stress_management "Stress management").[[76]](#cite_note-76) All practices vary depending upon the school and the teacher.

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sport")]

Karate is divided into style organizations.[[77]](#cite_note-google1-77) These organizations sometimes cooperate in non-style specific sport karate organizations or federations. Examples of sport organizations include AAKF/ITKF, AOK, TKL, AKA, WKF, NWUKO, WUKF and WKC.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Organizations hold competitions (tournaments) from local to international level. Tournaments are designed to match members of opposing schools or styles against one another in kata, sparring and weapons demonstration. They are often separated by age, rank and sex with potentially different rules or standards based on these factors. The tournament may be exclusively for members of a particular style (closed) or one in which any martial artist from any style may participate within the rules of the tournament (open).

The [World Karate Federation](/wiki/World_Karate_Federation "World Karate Federation") (WKF) is the largest sport karate organization and is recognized by the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) as being responsible for karate competition in the Olympic Games.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The WKF has developed common rules governing all styles. The national WKF organizations coordinate with their respective [National Olympic Committees](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committee "National Olympic Committee").

WKF karate competition has two disciplines: sparring (*kumite*) and forms (*[kata](/wiki/Karate_kata "Karate kata")*).[[80]](#cite_note-80) Competitors may enter either as individuals or as part of a team. Evaluation for kata and kobudō is performed by a panel of judges, whereas sparring is judged by a head referee, usually with assistant referees at the side of the sparring area. Sparring matches are typically divided by weight, age, gender, and experience.[[81]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-81)

WKF only allows membership through one national organization/federation per country to which clubs may join. The World Union of Karate-do Federations (WUKF)[[82]](#cite_note-82) offers different styles and federations a world body they may join, without having to compromise their style or size. The WUKF accepts more than one federation or association per country.

Sport organizations use different competition rule systems.[[77]](#cite_note-google1-77)[[81]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-81)[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) [Light contact](/wiki/Light_contact "Light contact") rules are used by the WKF, WUKO, IASK and WKC. [Full contact karate](/wiki/Full_contact_karate "Full contact karate") rules used by [Kyokushinkai](/wiki/Kyokushinkai "Kyokushinkai"), [Seidokaikan](/wiki/Seidokaikan "Seidokaikan") and other organizations. [Bogu kumite](/wiki/Bogu_kumite "Bogu kumite") (full contact with protective shielding of targets) rules are used in the World Koshiki Karate-Do Federation organization.[[86]](#cite_note-86) Shinkaratedo Federation use boxing gloves.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Within the United States, rules may be under the jurisdiction of state sports authorities, such as the boxing commission.

### Mixed Martial Arts (MMA)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Mixed Martial Arts (MMA)")]

Karate, although not widely used in [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts"), has been effective for some MMA practitioners.[[88]](#cite_note-karate_UFCb-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) Various styles of karate are practiced in MMA: [Lyoto Machida](/wiki/Lyoto_Machida "Lyoto Machida") and [John Makdessi](/wiki/John_Makdessi "John Makdessi") practice [Shotokan](/wiki/Shotokan "Shotokan");[[90]](#cite_note-90) [Bas Rutten](/wiki/Bas_Rutten "Bas Rutten") and [Georges St-Pierre](/wiki/Georges_St-Pierre "Georges St-Pierre") train in [Kyokushin](/wiki/Kyokushin "Kyokushin");[[91]](#cite_note-knucklepit-91) [Michelle Waterson](/wiki/Michelle_Waterson "Michelle Waterson") holds a [black belt](/wiki/Black_Belt_(martial_arts) "Black Belt (martial arts)") in [American Free Style Karate](/wiki/American_Karate "American Karate");[[92]](#cite_note-92) [Stephen Thompson](/wiki/Stephen_Thompson_(fighter) "Stephen Thompson (fighter)") practices [American Tetsushin Ryu Kempo Karate](/wiki/American_Kenpo_Karate "American Kenpo Karate") and both [Gunnar Nelson](/wiki/Gunnar_Nelson_(fighter) "Gunnar Nelson (fighter)")[[93]](#cite_note-93) and [Robert Whittaker](/wiki/Robert_Whittaker_(fighter) "Robert Whittaker (fighter)") practiced [Gōjū-ryū](/wiki/G%C5%8Dj%C5%AB-ry%C5%AB "Gōjū-ryū").[[94]](#cite_note-94) Additionally, [John Kavanagh](/wiki/John_Kavanagh_(martial_artist) "John Kavanagh (martial artist)") has been successful as coach with a [Kenpo Karate](/wiki/Kenp%C5%8D#Okinawan_Kenpo "Kenpō") pedigree.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

### Olympic Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Olympic Games")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/2018-10-18_Karate_Boys%27_%2B68_kg_at_2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Bronze_IRL%E2%80%93MAR_%28Martin_Rulsch%29_18.jpg/250px-2018-10-18_Karate_Boys%27_%2B68_kg_at_2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Bronze_IRL%E2%80%93MAR_%28Martin_Rulsch%29_18.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018-10-18_Karate_Boys%27_%2B68_kg_at_2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Bronze_IRL%E2%80%93MAR_(Martin_Rulsch)_18.jpg)

Bronze medal match at the [2018 Summer Youth Olympics](/wiki/2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics "2018 Summer Youth Olympics") in Buenos Aires, Argentina.

In August 2016, the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") approved [karate as an Olympic sport](/wiki/Karate_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Karate at the Summer Olympics") beginning at the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics").[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97) Karate also debuted at the [2018 Summer Youth Olympics](/wiki/2018_Summer_Youth_Olympics "2018 Summer Youth Olympics"). During this debut of Karate in the Summer Olympics, sixty competitors from around the world competed in the Kumite competition, and twenty competed in the Kata competition. In September 2015, karate was included in a shortlist along with [baseball](/wiki/Baseball_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Baseball at the Summer Olympics"), softball, [skateboarding](/wiki/Skateboarding_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Skateboarding at the Summer Olympics"), [surfing](/wiki/Surfing_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Surfing at the Summer Olympics"), and [sport climbing](/wiki/Sport_climbing_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Sport climbing at the Summer Olympics") to be considered for inclusion in the 2020 Summer Olympics;[[98]](#cite_note-98) and in June 2016, the executive board of the International Olympic Committee (IOC) announced that they would support the proposal to include all of the shortlisted sports in the 2020 Games.[[99]](#cite_note-99) Finally, on 3 August 2016, all five sports (counting baseball and softball together as one sport) were approved for inclusion in the 2020 Olympic program.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

Karate was not included in the [2024 Olympic Games](/wiki/2024_Olympic_Games "2024 Olympic Games"), although it has made the shortlist for inclusion, alongside nine others, in the [2028 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics").[[101]](#cite_note-101)

## Rank system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Rank system")]

See also: [Kyū](/wiki/Ky%C5%AB "Kyū")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/JJS_Dojo.jpg/250px-JJS_Dojo.jpg)](/wiki/File:JJS_Dojo.jpg)

Karatekas wearing different colored belts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/1gB.jpg/250px-1gB.jpg)](/wiki/File:1gB.jpg)

Karatekas at a [dojo](/wiki/Dojo "Dojo") with different colored belts

In 1924, Gichin Funakoshi, founder of Shotokan Karate, adopted the [Dan](/wiki/Dan_rank "Dan rank") system from the judo founder [Jigoro Kano](/wiki/Jigoro_Kano "Jigoro Kano")[[102]](#cite_note-102) using a [rank scheme](/wiki/Judo#Rank_and_grading "Judo") with a limited set of belt colors. Other Okinawan teachers also adopted this practice. In the [Kyū](/wiki/Ky%C5%AB "Kyū")/[Dan](/wiki/Dan_rank "Dan rank") system the beginner grades start with a higher numbered kyū (*e.g.*, 10th Kyū or Jukyū) and progress toward a lower numbered kyū. The Dan progression continues from 1st Dan (Shodan, or 'beginning dan') to the higher dan grades. Kyū-grade karateka are referred to as "color belt" or mudansha ("ones without dan/rank"). Dan-grade karateka are referred to as *yudansha* (holders of dan/rank). Yudansha typically wear a [black belt](/wiki/Black_belt_(martial_arts) "Black belt (martial arts)"). Normally, the first five to six dans are given by examination by superior dan holders, while the subsequent (7 and up) are honorary, given for special merits and/or age reached. Requirements of rank differ among styles, organizations, and schools. Kyū ranks stress [Karate stances](/wiki/Karate_stances "Karate stances"), [Equilibrioception](/wiki/Equilibrioception "Equilibrioception"), and [motor coordination](/wiki/Motor_coordination "Motor coordination"). Speed and power are added at higher grades.

Minimum age and time in rank are factors affecting promotion. Testing consists of demonstration of techniques before a panel of examiners or senseis. This will vary by school, but testing may include everything learned at that point, or just new information. The demonstration is an application for new rank (shinsa) and may include basics, [kata](/wiki/Kata "Kata"), [bunkai](/wiki/Bunkai "Bunkai"), self-defense, routines, [tameshiwari](/wiki/Tameshiwari "Tameshiwari") (breaking), and kumite (sparring).

## Philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Philosophy")]

In *Karate-Do Kyohan,* Funakoshi quoted from the [Heart Sutra](/wiki/Heart_Sutra "Heart Sutra"), which is prominent in [Shingon](/wiki/Shingon "Shingon") Buddhism: "Form is emptiness, emptiness is form itself" (*shiki zokuze kū kū zokuze shiki*).[[103]](#cite_note-103)
He interpreted the "kara" of Karate-dō to mean "to purge oneself of selfish and evil thoughts ... for only with a clear mind and conscience can the practitioner understand the knowledge which he receives." Funakoshi believed that one should be "inwardly humble and outwardly gentle." Only by behaving humbly can one be open to Karate's many lessons. This is done by listening and being receptive to criticism. He considered courtesy of prime importance. He said that "Karate is properly applied only in those rare situations in which one really must either down another or be downed by him." Funakoshi did not consider it unusual for a devotee to use Karate in a real physical confrontation no more than perhaps once in a lifetime. He stated that Karate practitioners must "never be easily drawn into a fight." It is understood that one blow from a real expert could mean death. It is clear that those who misuse what they have learned bring dishonor upon themselves. He promoted the character trait of personal conviction. In "time of grave public crisis, one must have the courage ... to face a million and one opponents." He taught that indecisiveness is a weakness.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

## Styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Styles")]

See also: [Comparison of karate styles](/wiki/Comparison_of_karate_styles "Comparison of karate styles")

Karate is divided into many styles, each with their different training methods, focuses, and cultures; though they mainly originate from the historical Okinawan parent styles of [Naha-te](/wiki/Okinawan_martial_arts#Naha-te "Okinawan martial arts"), [Tomari-te](/wiki/Okinawan_martial_arts#Tomari-te "Okinawan martial arts") and [Shuri-te](/wiki/Okinawan_martial_arts#Shuri-te "Okinawan martial arts").

However some of the schools' founders have been sceptical about the division of karate into many styles. [Gichin Funakoshi](/wiki/Gichin_Funakoshi "Gichin Funakoshi") simply stated that there are as many styles as instructors in the world while [Kenwa Mabuni](/wiki/Kenwa_Mabuni "Kenwa Mabuni") explained that the notion of different variations of karate came from outsiders.[[105]](#cite_note-105) Even before the popularization of karate in mainland Japan, the idea existed that karate was divided into two branches:[[106]](#cite_note-106) [Shōrei-ryū](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Drei-ry%C5%AB "Shōrei-ryū"), derived from Naha-te,[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-blackbelt1990-108) and [Shōrin-ryū](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Drin-ry%C5%AB "Shōrin-ryū"), drawing on Shuri-te and Tomari-te.[[50]](#cite_note-Higaonna_1985_19-50)[[109]](#cite_note-109) but [Chōjun Miyagi](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Djun_Miyagi "Chōjun Miyagi") believed that was just an incorrect perception.[[110]](#cite_note-110) [Kanken Tōyama](/wiki/Kanken_T%C5%8Dyama "Kanken Tōyama") disregarded the idea as he supported the "non-school principle".[[111]](#cite_note-111) [Mas Oyama](/wiki/Mas_Oyama "Mas Oyama") was actively opposed to the idea of the fragmentation into several karate schools.[[112]](#cite_note-112) He believed that making karate a [combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport"), as well keeping it as a martial art, could be a possible approach to unify all schools.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

By the 1980s, the four main schools of karate in mainland Japan were considered to be [Gōjū-ryū](/wiki/G%C5%8Dj%C5%AB-ry%C5%AB "Gōjū-ryū"), [Shotokan](/wiki/Shotokan "Shotokan"), [Shitō-ryū](/wiki/Shit%C5%8D-ry%C5%AB "Shitō-ryū"), and [Wadō-ryū](/wiki/Wad%C5%8D-ry%C5%AB "Wadō-ryū").[[114]](#cite_note-Higaonna_1985_21-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115) These four styles are those recognised by the World Karate Federation for international kata competition.[[116]](#cite_note-EssentialKarate2015-116) Some widespread styles[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[108]](#cite_note-blackbelt1990-108) often accepted for kata competition include [Kyokushin](/wiki/Kyokushin "Kyokushin"), [Shōrin-ryū](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Drin-ry%C5%AB "Shōrin-ryū"), [Uechi-Ryū](/wiki/Uechi-Ry%C5%AB "Uechi-Ryū") or [Isshin-ryū](/wiki/Isshin-ry%C5%AB "Isshin-ryū") among others.[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[116]](#cite_note-EssentialKarate2015-116)

## World

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: World")]

Karate has grown in popularity in Africa, particularly in South Africa and [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana").[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

### Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Americas")]

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Canada")]

Karate began in Canada in the 1930s and 1940s as Japanese people immigrated to the country. Karate was practised quietly without a large amount of organization. During the Second World War, many Japanese-Canadian families were moved to the interior of British Columbia. [Masaru Shintani](/wiki/Masaru_Shintani "Masaru Shintani"), at the age of 13, began to study Shorin-Ryu karate in the Japanese camp under Kitigawa. In 1956, after 9 years of training with Kitigawa, Shintani travelled to Japan and met [Hironori Otsuka](/wiki/Hironori_Otsuka "Hironori Otsuka") ([Wado Ryu](/wiki/Wado_Ryu "Wado Ryu")). In 1958, Otsuka invited Shintani to join his organization Wado Kai, and in 1969 he asked Shintani to officially call his style Wado.[[123]](#cite_note-JAMA-123)

In Canada during this same time, karate was also introduced by [Masami Tsuruoka](/wiki/Masami_Tsuruoka "Masami Tsuruoka") who had studied in Japan in the 1940s under [Tsuyoshi Chitose](/wiki/Tsuyoshi_Chitose "Tsuyoshi Chitose").[[124]](#cite_note-historica-124) In 1954, Tsuruoka initiated the first karate competition in Canada and laid the foundation for the [National Karate Association](/wiki/Karate_Canada "Karate Canada").[[124]](#cite_note-historica-124)

In the late 1950s Shintani moved to Ontario and began teaching karate and judo at the Japanese Cultural Centre in Hamilton. In 1966, he began (with Otsuka's endorsement) the Shintani Wado Kai Karate Federation. During the 1970s Otsuka appointed Shintani the Supreme Instructor of Wado Kai in North America. In 1979, Otsuka publicly promoted Shintani to hachidan (8th dan) and privately gave him a kudan certificate (9th dan), which was revealed by Shintani in 1995. Shintani and Otsuka visited each other in Japan and Canada several times, the last time in 1980 two years prior to Otsuka's death. Shintani died 7 May 2000.[[123]](#cite_note-JAMA-123)

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Karate in the United States](/wiki/Karate_in_the_United_States "Karate in the United States")

After World War II, members of the [United States military](/wiki/United_States_military "United States military") learned karate in Okinawa or Japan and then opened schools in the US. In 1945, [Robert Trias](/wiki/Robert_Trias "Robert Trias") opened the first *dōjō* in the United States in [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), a Shuri-ryū karate *dōjō*.[[125]](#cite_note-125) In the 1950s, [William J. Dometrich](/wiki/William_J._Dometrich "William J. Dometrich"), [Ed Parker](/wiki/Ed_Parker "Ed Parker"), [Cecil T. Patterson](/wiki/Cecil_T._Patterson "Cecil T. Patterson"), [Gordon Doversola](/wiki/Gordon_Doversola "Gordon Doversola"), [Harold G. Long](/wiki/Harold_G._Long "Harold G. Long"), [Donald Hugh Nagle](/wiki/Donald_Hugh_Nagle "Donald Hugh Nagle"), [George Mattson](/wiki/George_Mattson_(martial_artist) "George Mattson (martial artist)") and [Peter Urban](/wiki/Peter_Urban_(karate) "Peter Urban (karate)") all began instructing in the US.

[Tsutomu Ohshima](/wiki/Tsutomu_Ohshima "Tsutomu Ohshima") began studying karate under Shotokan's founder, Gichin Funakoshi, while a student at Waseda University, beginning in 1948. In 1957, Ohshima received his godan (fifth-degree black belt), the highest rank awarded by Funakoshi. He founded the first university karate club in the United States at [California Institute of Technology](/wiki/California_Institute_of_Technology "California Institute of Technology") in 1957. In 1959, he founded the Southern California Karate Association (SCKA) which was renamed [Shotokan Karate of America](/wiki/Shotokan_Karate_of_America "Shotokan Karate of America") (SKA) in 1969.

In the 1960s, Anthony Mirakian, [Richard Kim](/wiki/Richard_Kim_(karate) "Richard Kim (karate)"), [Teruyuki Okazaki](/wiki/Teruyuki_Okazaki "Teruyuki Okazaki"), [John Pachivas](/wiki/John_Pachivas "John Pachivas"), [Allen Steen](/wiki/Allen_Steen "Allen Steen"), Gosei Yamaguchi (son of [Gōgen Yamaguchi](/wiki/G%C5%8Dgen_Yamaguchi "Gōgen Yamaguchi")), [Michael G. Foster](/wiki/Michael_G._Foster "Michael G. Foster") and Pat Burleson began teaching martial arts around the country.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

In 1961, [Hidetaka Nishiyama](/wiki/Hidetaka_Nishiyama "Hidetaka Nishiyama"), a co-founder of the [Japan Karate Association](/wiki/Japan_Karate_Association "Japan Karate Association") (JKA) and student of Gichin Funakoshi, began teaching in the United States. He founded the International Traditional Karate Federation (ITKF). [Takayuki Mikami](/wiki/Takayuki_Mikami "Takayuki Mikami") was sent to New Orleans by the JKA in 1963.

In 1964, [Takayuki Kubota](/wiki/Takayuki_Kubota "Takayuki Kubota") relocated the International Karate Association from Tokyo to California.

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Asia")]

#### Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Korea")]

See also: [Korea under Japanese rule](/wiki/Korea_under_Japanese_rule "Korea under Japanese rule")

Due to past conflict between Korea and Japan, most notably during the [Japanese occupation of Korea](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_Korea "Japanese occupation of Korea") in the early 20th century, the influence of karate in Korea is a contentious issue.[[127]](#cite_note-127) From 1910 until 1945, Korea was annexed by the Japanese Empire. It was during this time that many of the Korean martial arts masters of the 20th century were exposed to Japanese karate. After regaining independence from Japan, many Korean martial arts schools that opened up in the 1940s and 1950s were founded by masters who had trained in karate in Japan as part of their martial arts training.

[Won Kuk Lee](/wiki/Won_Kuk_Lee "Won Kuk Lee"), a Korean student of Funakoshi, founded the first martial arts school after the Japanese occupation of Korea ended in 1945, called the [Chung Do Kwan](/wiki/Chung_Do_Kwan "Chung Do Kwan"). Having studied under [Gichin Funakoshi](/wiki/Gichin_Funakoshi "Gichin Funakoshi") at [Chuo University](/wiki/Chuo_University "Chuo University"), Lee had incorporated [taekkyon](/wiki/Taekkyon "Taekkyon"), [kung fu](/wiki/Kung_fu "Kung fu"), and karate in the martial art that he taught which he called "[Tang Soo Do](/wiki/Tang_Soo_Do "Tang Soo Do")", the Korean transliteration of the Chinese characters for "Way of Chinese Hand" (唐手道).[[128]](#cite_note-128) In the mid-1950s, the martial arts schools were unified under President [Rhee Syngman](/wiki/Rhee_Syngman "Rhee Syngman")'s order, and became [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo") under the leadership of [Choi Hong Hi](/wiki/Choi_Hong_Hi "Choi Hong Hi") and a committee of Korean masters. Choi, a significant figure in taekwondo history, had also studied karate under Funakoshi. Karate also provided an important comparative model for the early founders of taekwondo in the formalization of their art including [hyung](/wiki/Hyeong "Hyeong") and the [belt ranking](/wiki/Taekwondo#Ranks,_belts,_and_promotion "Taekwondo") system. The original taekwondo *hyung* were identical to karate *[kata](/wiki/Kata "Kata")*. Eventually, original Korean forms were developed by individual schools and associations. Although the [World Taekwondo Federation](/wiki/World_Taekwondo_Federation "World Taekwondo Federation") and [International Taekwon-Do Federation](/wiki/International_Taekwon-Do_Federation "International Taekwon-Do Federation") are the most prominent among Korean martial arts organizations, *tang soo do* schools that teach Japanese karate still exist as they were originally conveyed to Won Kuk Lee and his contemporaries from Funakoshi.

#### Soviet Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Soviet Union")]

Karate appeared in the Soviet Union in the mid-1960s, during [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev")'s policy of improved international relations. The first Shotokan clubs were opened in Moscow's universities.[[129]](#cite_note-129) In 1973, however, the government banned karate—together with all other foreign martial arts—endorsing only the Soviet martial art of [sambo](/wiki/Sambo_(martial_art) "Sambo (martial art)").[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131) Failing to suppress these uncontrolled groups, the USSR's Sport Committee formed the Karate Federation of USSR in December 1978.[[132]](#cite_note-132) On 17 May 1984, the Soviet Karate Federation was disbanded and all karate became illegal again. In 1989, karate practice became legal again, but under strict government regulations, only after the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") in 1991 did independent karate schools resume functioning, and so federations were formed and national tournaments in authentic styles began.[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134)
