### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Karate_in_Lappeenranta%2C_Finland.jpg/250px-Karate_in_Lappeenranta%2C_Finland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karate_in_Lappeenranta,_Finland.jpg)

Karate movements in [Lappeenranta](/wiki/Lappeenranta "Lappeenranta")

In the 1950s and 1960s, several Japanese karate masters began to teach the art in Europe, but it was not until 1965 that the Japan Karate Association (JKA) sent to Europe four well-trained young Karate instructors [Taiji Kase](/wiki/Taiji_Kase "Taiji Kase"), [Keinosuke Enoeda](/wiki/Keinosuke_Enoeda "Keinosuke Enoeda"), [Hirokazu Kanazawa](/wiki/Hirokazu_Kanazawa "Hirokazu Kanazawa") and [Hiroshi Shirai](/wiki/Hiroshi_Shirai "Hiroshi Shirai").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Kase went to France, Enoeada to England and Shirai in Italy. These Masters maintained always a strong link between them, the JKA and the others JKA masters in the world, especially [Hidetaka Nishiyama](/wiki/Hidetaka_Nishiyama "Hidetaka Nishiyama") in the US

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: France")]

France Shotokan Karate was created in 1964 by Tsutomu Ohshima. It is affiliated with another of his organizations, Shotokan Karate of America (SKA). However, in 1965 [Taiji Kase](/wiki/Taiji_Kase "Taiji Kase") came from Japan along with Enoeda and Shirai, who went to England and Italy respectively, and karate came under the influence of the JKA.

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Italy")]

[Hiroshi Shirai](/wiki/Hiroshi_Shirai "Hiroshi Shirai"), one of the original instructors sent by the JKA to Europe along with Kase, Enoeda and Kanazawa, moved to Italy in 1965 and quickly established a Shotokan enclave that spawned several instructors who in their turn soon spread the style all over the country. By 1970 Shotokan karate was the most spread martial art in Italy apart from Judo. Other styles such as [Wado Ryu](/wiki/Wado_Ryu "Wado Ryu"), [Goju Ryu](/wiki/Goju_Ryu "Goju Ryu") and [Shito Ryu](/wiki/Shito_Ryu "Shito Ryu"), are present and well established in Italy, while [Shotokan](/wiki/Shotokan "Shotokan") remains the most popular.

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main article: [Karate in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Karate_in_the_United_Kingdom "Karate in the United Kingdom")

[Vernon Bell](/wiki/Vernon_Bell "Vernon Bell"), a 3rd Dan Judo instructor who had been instructed by [Kenshiro Abbe](/wiki/Kenshiro_Abbe "Kenshiro Abbe") introduced Karate to England in 1956, having attended classes in [Henry Plée](/wiki/Henry_Pl%C3%A9e "Henry Plée")'s [Yoseikan](/wiki/Yoseikan "Yoseikan") *dōjō* in Paris. Yoseikan had been founded by [Minoru Mochizuki](/wiki/Minoru_Mochizuki "Minoru Mochizuki"), a master of multiple Japanese martial arts, who had studied Karate with [Gichin Funakoshi](/wiki/Gichin_Funakoshi "Gichin Funakoshi"), thus the Yoseikan style was heavily influenced by Shotokan.[[135]](#cite_note-UK_History-135) Bell began teaching in the tennis courts of his parents' back garden in Ilford, Essex and his group was to become the British Karate Federation. On 19 July 1957, Vietnamese Hoang Nam 3rd Dan, billed as "Karate champion of Indo China", was invited to teach by Bell at Maybush Road, but the first instructor from Japan was [Tetsuji Murakami](/wiki/Tetsuji_Murakami "Tetsuji Murakami") (1927–1987) a 3rd Dan Yoseikan under Minoru Mochizuki and 1st Dan of the JKA, who arrived in England in July 1959.[[135]](#cite_note-UK_History-135) In 1959, Frederick Gille set up the Liverpool branch of the British Karate Federation, which was officially recognised in 1961. The Liverpool branch was based at Harold House Jewish Boys Club in Chatham Street before relocating to the YMCA in Everton where it became known as the Red Triangle. One of the early members of this branch was [Andy Sherry](/wiki/Andy_Sherry "Andy Sherry") who had previously studied Jujutsu with Jack Britten. In 1961, Edward Ainsworth, another blackbelt Judoka, set up the first Karate study group in [Ayrshire](/wiki/Ayrshire "Ayrshire"), Scotland having attended Bell's third 'Karate Summer School' in 1961.[[135]](#cite_note-UK_History-135)

Outside Bell's organisation, Charles Mack traveled to Japan and studied under [Masatoshi Nakayama](/wiki/Masatoshi_Nakayama "Masatoshi Nakayama") of the [Japan Karate Association](/wiki/Japan_Karate_Association "Japan Karate Association") who graded Mack to 1st Dan Shotokan on 4 March 1962 in Japan.[[135]](#cite_note-UK_History-135) [Shotokai](/wiki/Shotokai "Shotokai") Karate was introduced to England in 1963 by another of [Gichin Funakoshi](/wiki/Gichin_Funakoshi "Gichin Funakoshi")'s students, [Mitsusuke Harada](/wiki/Mitsusuke_Harada "Mitsusuke Harada").[[135]](#cite_note-UK_History-135) Outside the Shotokan stable of karate styles, [Wado Ryu](/wiki/Wado_Ryu "Wado Ryu") Karate was also an early adopted style in the UK, introduced by [Tatsuo Suzuki](/wiki/Tatsuo_Suzuki_(martial_artist) "Tatsuo Suzuki (martial artist)"), a 6th Dan at the time in 1964.

Despite the early adoption of Shotokan in the UK, it was not until 1964 that JKA Shotokan officially came to the UK. Bell had been corresponding with the JKA in Tokyo asking for his grades to be ratified in Shotokan having apparently learnt that Murakami was not a designated representative of the JKA. The JKA obliged, and without enforcing a grading on Bell, ratified his black belt on 5 February 1964, though he had to relinquish his Yoseikan grade. Bell requested a visitation from JKA instructors and the next year [Taiji Kase](/wiki/Taiji_Kase "Taiji Kase"), [Hirokazu Kanazawa](/wiki/Hirokazu_Kanazawa "Hirokazu Kanazawa"), [Keinosuke Enoeda](/wiki/Keinosuke_Enoeda "Keinosuke Enoeda") and [Hiroshi Shirai](/wiki/Hiroshi_Shirai "Hiroshi Shirai") gave the first JKA demo at the old [Kensington Town Hall](/wiki/Old_Town_Hall,_Kensington "Old Town Hall, Kensington") on 21 April 1965. [Hirokazu Kanazawa](/wiki/Hirokazu_Kanazawa "Hirokazu Kanazawa") and [Keinosuke Enoeda](/wiki/Keinosuke_Enoeda "Keinosuke Enoeda") stayed and Murakami left (later re-emerging as a 5th Dan Shotokai under Harada).[[135]](#cite_note-UK_History-135)

In 1966, members of the former British Karate Federation established the [Karate Union of Great Britain](/wiki/Karate_Union_of_Great_Britain "Karate Union of Great Britain") (KUGB) under [Hirokazu Kanazawa](/wiki/Hirokazu_Kanazawa "Hirokazu Kanazawa") as chief instructor[[136]](#cite_note-136) and affiliated to JKA. [Keinosuke Enoeda](/wiki/Keinosuke_Enoeda "Keinosuke Enoeda") came to England at the same time as Kanazawa, teaching at a *dōjō* in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"). Kanazawa left the UK after 3 years and Enoeda took over. After Enoeda's death in 2003, the KUGB elected Andy Sherry as Chief Instructor. Shortly after this, a new association split off from KUGB, [JKA England](/wiki/JKA_England "JKA England").
An earlier significant split from the KUGB took place in 1991 when a group led by KUGB senior instructor Steve Cattle formed the English Shotokan Academy (ESA). The aim of this group was to follow the teachings of [Taiji Kase](/wiki/Taiji_Kase "Taiji Kase"), formerly the JKA chief instructor in Europe, who along with Hiroshi Shirai created the World Shotokan Karate-do Academy (WKSA), in 1989 to pursue the teaching of "Budo" karate as opposed to what he viewed as "sport karate". Kase sought to return the practice of Shotokan Karate to its martial roots, reintroducing among other things open hand and throwing techniques that had been side lined as the result of competition rules introduced by the JKA. Both the ESA and the WKSA (renamed the Kase-Ha Shotokan-Ryu Karate-do Academy (KSKA) after Kase's death in 2004) continue following this path today.
In 1975, Great Britain became the first team ever to take the World male team title from Japan after being defeated the previous year in the final.

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Oceania")]

The [World Karate Federation](/wiki/World_Karate_Federation "World Karate Federation") was first introduced to Oceania as the [Oceanian Karate Federation](/wiki/Oceanian_Karate_Federation "Oceanian Karate Federation") 1973.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Australia")]

The [Australian Karate Federation](/wiki/Australian_Karate_Federation "Australian Karate Federation"), under the World Karate Federation, was first introduced in 1970. In 1972 Frank Novak became the first fully qualified Shotokan instructor to arrive in Australia and teach in the country,[[138]](#cite_note-138) establishing the first Shotokan Karate dojo in Australia.[[139]](#cite_note-139) At karate's debut in the Olympics at the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics"), [Tsuneari Yahiro](/wiki/Tsuneari_Yahiro "Tsuneari Yahiro") became Australia's first Karate Olympian.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

## In film and popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karate&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: In film and popular culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Bruce_Lee_1973.jpg/250px-Bruce_Lee_1973.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bruce_Lee_1973.jpg)

[Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee") is known for practicing many martial arts styles, including Karate, having trained with [Chuck Norris](/wiki/Chuck_Norris "Chuck Norris") for many years.

Karate spread rapidly in the West through popular culture. In 1950s popular fiction, karate was at times described to readers in near-mythical terms, and it was credible to show Western experts of unarmed combat as unaware of Eastern martial arts of this kind.[[141]](#cite_note-141)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Following the [inclusion of judo](/wiki/Judo_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Judo at the Summer Olympics") at the [1964 Tokyo Olympics](/wiki/1964_Tokyo_Olympics "1964 Tokyo Olympics"), there was growing mainstream Western interest in [Japanese martial arts](/wiki/Japanese_martial_arts "Japanese martial arts"), particularly karate, during the 1960s.[[142]](#cite_note-142) By the 1970s, [martial arts films](/wiki/Martial_arts_films "Martial arts films") (especially [kung fu films](/wiki/Kung_fu_films "Kung fu films") and [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee") flicks [from Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_action_cinema "Hong Kong action cinema")) had formed a mainstream genre and launched the "[kung fu craze](/wiki/Chopsocky "Chopsocky")" which propelled karate and other [Asian martial arts](/wiki/Asian_martial_arts "Asian martial arts") into mass popularity. However, mainstream Western audiences at the time generally did not distinguish between different Asian martial arts such as karate, [kung fu](/wiki/Kung_fu "Kung fu") and [tae kwon do](/wiki/Tae_kwon_do "Tae kwon do").[[143]](#cite_note-karate_UFC-143)

In the [film series](/wiki/Film_series "Film series") [007](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") (1953–present), the main [protagonist](/wiki/Protagonist "Protagonist") [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond_(literary_character) "James Bond (literary character)") is exceptionally skillful in martial arts. He is an expert in various types of martial arts including Karate, as well as Judo, Aikido, Brazilian jiu-jitsu, Filipino Eskrima and Krav Maga.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the late 20th century, specifically during the 80s and 90s, karate saw a rise in mainstream popularity. America in the 80s took hold of the martial arts craze and began to produce more homegrown films in the [martial arts genre](/wiki/Martial_arts_film "Martial arts film").[[144]](#cite_note-144) Films weren't the only popular visual representation of Karate in the 80s, just as [arcades](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") grew in popularity, so did Karate in arcade [fighting games](/wiki/Fighting_game "Fighting game"). The first video game to feature fist fighting was *[Heavyweight Champ](/wiki/Heavyweight_Champ "Heavyweight Champ")* in 1976,[[145]](#cite_note-145) but it was *[Karate Champ](/wiki/Karate_Champ "Karate Champ")* that popularized the one-on-one fighting game genre in [arcades](/wiki/Amusement_arcade "Amusement arcade") in 1984. In 1987, [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom") released *[Street Fighter](/wiki/Street_Fighter_(video_game) "Street Fighter (video game)")*, featuring multiple Karateka characters.[[146]](#cite_note-aac320-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Street_Fighter_cosplayers_at_FanimeCon_2010-05-30_3.JPG/250px-Street_Fighter_cosplayers_at_FanimeCon_2010-05-30_3.JPG)](/wiki/File:Street_Fighter_cosplayers_at_FanimeCon_2010-05-30_3.JPG)

Cosplayers of Karateka characters from [Street Fighter](/wiki/Street_Fighter_(video_game) "Street Fighter (video game)")

*[The Karate Kid](/wiki/The_Karate_Kid_(1984_film) "The Karate Kid (1984 film)")* (1984) and its sequels *[The Karate Kid, Part II](/wiki/The_Karate_Kid,_Part_II "The Karate Kid, Part II")* (1986), *[The Karate Kid, Part III](/wiki/The_Karate_Kid,_Part_III "The Karate Kid, Part III")* (1989) and *[The Next Karate Kid](/wiki/The_Next_Karate_Kid "The Next Karate Kid")* (1994) are films relating the fictional story of an American adolescent's introduction into karate.[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149) Its television sequel, *[Cobra Kai](/wiki/Cobra_Kai "Cobra Kai")* (2018), has led to similar growing interest in karate.[[150]](#cite_note-150) The success of *The Karate Kid* further popularized karate (as opposed to Asian martial arts more generally) in mainstream American popular culture.[[143]](#cite_note-karate_UFC-143) *[Karate Kommandos](/wiki/Karate_Kommandos "Karate Kommandos")* is an animated children's show, with [Chuck Norris](/wiki/Chuck_Norris "Chuck Norris") appearing to reveal the moral lessons contained in every episode.
*[Dragon Ball](/wiki/Dragon_Ball "Dragon Ball")* (1984–present) is a Japanese [media franchise](/wiki/Media_franchise "Media franchise") ([Anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime")) whose characters use a variety and [hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_martial_arts "Hybrid martial arts") of [east Asian martial arts](/wiki/List_of_martial_arts#Asia "List of martial arts") styles, including Karate[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-:03-152)[[153]](#cite_note-:1-153) and [Wing Chun](/wiki/Wing_Chun "Wing Chun") ([Kung fu](/wiki/Kung_fu_(term) "Kung fu (term)")).[[152]](#cite_note-:03-152)[[153]](#cite_note-:1-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154) *Dragon Ball* was originally inspired by the classical 16th-century Chinese novel *[Journey to the West](/wiki/Journey_to_the_West "Journey to the West")*, combined with elements of [Hong Kong martial arts films](/wiki/Hong_Kong_martial_arts_films "Hong Kong martial arts films"), with influences of [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan") and [Bruce Lee](/wiki/Bruce_Lee "Bruce Lee").

In the film series [*The Matrix*](/wiki/The_Matrix_(franchise) "The Matrix (franchise)"), [Neo](/wiki/Neo_(The_Matrix) "Neo (The Matrix)") uses a variety of martial arts styles.[[155]](#cite_note-:02-155) Neo's skill in martial arts was shown as having been downloaded into his brain, which granted combat abilities equivalent to a martial artist with decades of experience. Kenpo Karate is one of the many styles Neo learns as part of his computerised combat training.[[156]](#cite_note-156) As part of the preparation for the movie, [Yuen Woo-ping](/wiki/Yuen_Woo-ping "Yuen Woo-ping") had [Keanu Reeves](/wiki/Keanu_Reeves "Keanu Reeves") undertake four months of martial arts training in a variety of different styles.[[155]](#cite_note-:02-155)
